title: Algebraic Flat Connections and O-Minimality
speaker: Hélène Esnault
abstract:
We prove that an algebraic flat connection has ℝan, exp
-definable flat sections if and only if it is regular singular with unitary monodromy eigenvalues at infinity, refining previous work of Bakker–Mullane. This provides e.g. an o-minimal characterization of classical properties of the *Gau{\ss}-Manin connection*. Joint with Moritz Kerz
*[I lectured on this in summer, two or three of the speakers were in attendance. In the mean time, there  has been modest progress, albeit some  little setback as well.]
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Classical Hodge-locus template
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a polarized integral variation of Hodge structure on a quasi-projective base and recalls the Cattani-Deligne-Kaplan algebraicity theorem via Schmid's algebraicity of the Hodge filtration, definability of flat sections, bounded finiteness, and definable GAGA.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian Simpson Hodge-locus analogue
proof_stages.summary: The speaker replaces the Gauss-Manin/VHS setting by relative Betti, de Rham, and Dolbeault moduli of flat or Higgs objects, formulates Simpson's conjecture that connected components of the nonabelian Hodge locus are algebraic, and identifies the analogues of the classical proof's algebraicity and finiteness inputs.

proof_stages.label: Definability criterion for algebraic flat connections
proof_stages.summary: The central o-minimal question is isolated: for an algebraic flat connection, local flat sections are definable exactly under boundary conditions, namely regular singularity together with monodromy-at-infinity eigenvalues of absolute value one.

proof_stages.label: Obstruction to naive definable moduli base change
proof_stages.summary: The nonabelian flat-section analogue is identified with isomonodromy sections of the de Rham moduli, but the speaker explains that the definable analogue of Simpson's analytic moduli/base-change theorem fails and that a Dolbeault-side reformulation may be needed.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to punctured disks and formal normal form
proof_stages.summary: To prove the flat-connection criterion, the problem is reduced by curve criteria, small definable opens, and ramification to a connection on a punctured disk, where the formal Levelt-Turrittin-type solution separates irregular exponential terms from residue data.

proof_stages.label: Multisummable lifts and rank-one oscillation exclusion
proof_stages.summary: Formal solutions are compared with analytic asymptotic or multisummable lifts on sectors, and after reduction to rank one the no-periodicity property of definable functions forces the irregular exponential polynomial to vanish and the residue eigenvalues to have the required real/unitary behavior.

examples.name: Deligne's one-point-base finiteness case
examples.note: The special case where the base is Spec(C) is used as a benchmark for the desired finiteness statement for variations of Hodge structure in families.
examples.engagement_reason: Deligne’s Spec C case is cited as the known finiteness theorem and motivation for the relative question, with no computation or construction carried out.

examples.name: Changing the complex structure on a fixed topological curve
examples.note: The speaker illustrates the hard family version of Deligne-type finiteness by asking whether finiteness persists when a curve's complex structure varies.

examples.name: Affine-line equation with exponential solution
examples.note: An audience question about an algebraic differential equation whose solution is exponential tests how the definability criterion rules out global complex-periodic behavior and detects irregularity at infinity.
examples.engagement_reason: The affine-line exponential-solution example is tested against definability/regularity, with the discussion noting exponential’s infinite fibers/fundamental-domain issue and that the connection is not regular at infinity.

examples.name: Constant elliptic curve over G_m with a moving definable line bundle
examples.note: This counterexample shows that the naive definable analogue of Simpson's analytic base-change statement fails, forcing a repair of the nonabelian strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the constant elliptic-curve-over-G_m counterexample using two opens, transition functions, a moving definable line bundle, and the induced Picard contradiction.
